era_context: The Middle Bronze Age in the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East. The Old Babylonian period is in its final decades under Hammurabi's successors; Egypt is in the Second Intermediate Period with the Hyksos presence in the north; the Hittite Old Kingdom is consolidating power in Anatolia; Mycenaean Greece is in the Early Bronze Age III; the Indus Valley Civilization is in its mature phase.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological dating uncertain)

events.title: Reign of Samsu-iluna, Hammurabi's successor, in Babylon
events.description: Samsu-iluna ruled Babylon following his father Hammurabi's death (traditionally dated c. 1750 BCE, though some scholars place it earlier). His reign was marked by military campaigns against the Kassites and other rivals, and by the gradual erosion of Babylonian territorial control. The kingdom faced increasing pressure from external enemies and internal fragmentation. His reign represents the transition from the height of Old Babylonian power to its decline.

sources.name: King List A and other Babylonian king lists
Cuneiform administrative and legal texts from Babylon

events.certainty_note: Samsu-iluna's reign is attested in cuneiform sources, but absolute dating of the Old Babylonian period remains debated. Conventional chronology places his reign c. 1749–1712 BCE, but some scholars argue for dates 50–100 years lower. The year -1755 falls within his reign under most chronologies.

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Hantili I or his successors
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom was consolidating territorial control in central Anatolia during this period. Following the reign of Hantili I (who overthrew the Amorite dynasty c. 1650 BCE in traditional chronology), the Hittites were expanding their influence and military capabilities. This era saw the development of Hittite administrative structures and the beginning of their emergence as a major Near Eastern power.
key_figures: Hantili I or his successors (Hittite rulers)

sources.name: Hittite royal annals and king lists (later compilations)
Archaeological evidence from Hattusa and other Hittite sites

events.certainty_note: Hittite chronology for this period is uncertain. The traditional dating of Hantili I is c. 1650 BCE, but some scholars place him earlier or later. The specific events of -1755 are not well-documented; this entry reflects the broader era of Hittite consolidation.

disconfirming_evidence: The absolute chronology of the Old Babylonian period is contested. Some scholars (particularly advocates of the 'low chronology') place Hammurabi's reign and that of his successors 50–150 years later than the conventional chronology used here. This affects the dating of Samsu-iluna's reign and all events attributed to this year. Additionally, the significance of events in Anatolia and the Hittite world for this specific year is largely inferential, as contemporary documentation is sparse.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -1755 is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia, where cuneiform texts provide administrative and legal records. Egypt's Second Intermediate Period is poorly documented in surviving texts, creating a significant gap in our understanding of the eastern Mediterranean. The Indus Valley Civilization, though contemporaneous and sophisticated, left no deciphered written records for this year. Hittite sources are later compilations, introducing chronological uncertainty. The historical record is dominated by Near Eastern court and administrative perspectives; the experiences of non-elite populations and regions outside the major power centers are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Samsu-iluna's reign continued the trajectory of Babylonian decline that would accelerate in subsequent decades
